sentences.base_language: It was Andrés's thirty-fifth birthday and his friends had been secretly planning a surprise party for weeks.
sentences.target_language: Era el trigésimo quinto cumpleaños de Andrés y sus amigos llevaban semanas planeando en secreto una fiesta sorpresa.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Llevaban semanas planeando** — **llevar + time expression + gerundio** expresses an action that has been ongoing for a period of time up to the present or narrative moment: **llevaban semanas planeando** (had been planning for weeks). This construction is the natural Spanish equivalent of English 'had been doing for [time]' and is far more idiomatic than a literal pluperfect continuous. Compare: **hacía semanas que planeaban** (it had been weeks since they were planning — also correct but less vivid).
**En secreto** = secretly — a fixed adverbial phrase: **planeaban en secreto** (were planning in secret / secretly). Compare: **en voz baja** (quietly), **en silencio** (in silence). **Fiesta sorpresa** = surprise party — **sorpresa** functions as a noun modifier after **fiesta**, similar to **visita sorpresa** (surprise visit) or **regalo sorpresa** (surprise gift). Note that in Spanish, noun-noun compounds of this type require no hyphen and place the modifier after the head noun.

sentences.base_language: He spent the morning convinced it would be a quiet day — he had told everyone he did not want any fuss.
sentences.target_language: Pasó la mañana convencido de que sería un día tranquilo — les había dicho a todos que no quería ningún alboroto.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Convencido de que + condicional** — the adjective **convencido** (convinced) triggers **de que** before a subordinate clause. The conditional **sería** (would be) follows sequence-of-tenses rules: from Andrés's morning perspective, the quiet day lay in the future, so the conditional is used rather than the present: **creía que sería tranquilo** (he thought it would be quiet). This is the same shift seen in reported speech: future → conditional after past anchor.
**Les había dicho a todos que no quería** — pluperfect in reported speech: **había dicho** (had told) places the telling earlier than the narrative morning. The double marking **les... a todos** (to them... to everyone) is standard Spanish: the indirect object pronoun **les** is obligatory even when the noun **a todos** is present. **Ningún alboroto** = no fuss — **ningún** (no, not any) precedes masculine singular nouns and conveys emphatic negation; **alboroto** = commotion, fuss, noise. Compare: **ningún problema** (no problem), **ninguna molestia** (no bother).

sentences.base_language: His closest friend called in the afternoon and casually suggested coming over for a simple dinner together.
sentences.target_language: Su amiga más cercana lo llamó por la tarde y, con naturalidad, le propuso que pasara a cenar algo sencillo juntos.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Le propuso que pasara** — verb of suggestion + subjunctive: **proponer que + subjuntivo** = to suggest that (someone do something). After **proponer** (to propose, to suggest), the subordinate clause requires the present or past subjunctive depending on the tense of the main verb: here **propuso** (preterite) triggers the past subjunctive **pasara**. Compare: **sugirió que viniera** (suggested he come), **propuso que lo dejaran** (proposed they drop it).
**Con naturalidad** = casually, naturally, without making it seem deliberate — **con + abstract noun** is a highly productive structure for manner adverbials: **con naturalidad** (casually, naturally), **con cuidado** (carefully), **con entusiasmo** (enthusiastically). **Pasar a cenar** = to come over for dinner, to stop by for dinner — **pasar** (to come over, to stop in) + **a + infinitivo** expresses the purpose of the visit: **pasa a verme** (come see me), **pasó a recogerlo** (stopped by to pick it up). This usage of **pasar** is very common in colloquial spoken Spanish.

sentences.base_language: When Andrés arrived and the door opened, a room full of people shouted 'Surprise!' all at once.
sentences.target_language: Cuando Andrés llegó y se abrió la puerta, una sala llena de gente gritó «¡Sorpresa!» al mismo tiempo.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Se abrió la puerta** — reflexive passive (pasiva refleja): **se + third person verb** is used when the agent (who opened the door) is unimportant or unknown. Compare: **alguien abrió la puerta** (someone opened the door — active, agent explicit) and **la puerta fue abierta** (the door was opened — passive with **ser**, more formal/written). The reflexive passive with **se** is the most natural spoken and written choice for inanimate subjects in Spanish.
**Al mismo tiempo** = at the same time, all at once — a fixed adverbial phrase: **gritaron al mismo tiempo** (shouted at the same time). Compare: **a la vez** (at the same time — fully interchangeable in most contexts): **todos hablaron a la vez** (everyone spoke at once). **Una sala llena de gente** — **lleno/a de** = full of: **llena de gente** (full of people). Note **sala** (living room, room) rather than **salón** (sitting room — slightly more formal) or **habitación** (room, bedroom); **sala** is the most neutral and common term for the main room of a home in Latin American Spanish.

sentences.base_language: His heart leaped and he felt tears spring to his eyes — he had had absolutely no idea.
sentences.target_language: El corazón le dio un vuelco y sintió que los ojos se le llenaban de lágrimas — no había tenido ni la más mínima idea.
sentences.grammarNote:
**El corazón le dio un vuelco** — gustar-type structure with **dar un vuelco**: the heart is the grammatical subject and Andrés is the indirect object **le**: **el corazón le dio un vuelco** (his heart leaped / gave a lurch). **Dar un vuelco** (lit. to give a tumble) = to leap, to lurch — used for the heart reacting to shock or emotion: **el corazón me dio un vuelco** (my heart jumped). The indirect object **le** takes the place of a possessive: compare *su corazón* (less idiomatic) vs. **el corazón le dio un vuelco** (natural and idiomatic).
**No había tenido ni la más mínima idea** — pluperfect with emphatic negation: **no había tenido** (had not had) places the ignorance before the moment of the party. **Ni la más mínima idea** = absolutely no idea, not the slightest clue — a fixed intensifying phrase: **ni** + superlative + noun for rhetorical emphasis. Compare: **no tenía ni idea** (had no idea — imperfect, more neutral), **ni la más remota idea** (not the faintest idea — synonymous). **Los ojos se le llenaban de lágrimas** — **llenarse de** (to fill with) + indirect object: **se le llenaban los ojos** (his eyes were filling) uses the same indirect object pattern.

sentences.base_language: The living room was decorated with flowers and colourful lights, and his favourite music was already playing.
sentences.target_language: La sala estaba decorada con flores y luces de colores, y su música favorita ya estaba sonando.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Estaba decorada** — **estar + past participle** describes the resulting state of a decoration, not the act itself: **estaba decorada** (was decorated — in a decorated state). Compare: **fue decorada** (was decorated — passive action with **ser**, emphasizing when the decorating happened). For describing the appearance of a space at a given moment, **estar + participio** is always the correct choice. The participle **decorada** agrees with **la sala** (feminine singular).
**Ya estaba sonando** — past progressive (**estaba + gerundio**) emphasizes that the music was already in progress when Andrés arrived: **ya estaba sonando** (was already playing). Compare: **ya sonaba** (imperfect — was already sounding, more neutral background). The past progressive **estaba sonando** highlights the vividness and immediacy of the ongoing action. **Luces de colores** = colourful lights — **de colores** (of colours, colourful) is a fixed modifier: **globos de colores** (colourful balloons), **cintas de colores** (colourful ribbons).

sentences.base_language: Friends from every stage of his life were there — old schoolmates, work colleagues, cousins he had not seen in years.
sentences.target_language: Había amigos de todas las etapas de su vida — viejos compañeros del colegio, colegas del trabajo, primos a los que no había visto en años.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Había amigos** — existential **haber** in the imperfect: **había** (there were) is the impersonal form of **haber** used to express existence or presence. The noun that follows is the grammatical object, so **haber** never agrees with it in standard usage: **había amigos** (there were friends), not *habían amigos*. This is a common error; **haber** in this impersonal use remains invariably third-person singular.
**Primos a los que no había visto en años** — relative clause with a preposition: **a los que** introduces a relative clause where the antecedent (**primos**) is the direct object of **ver** and requires **a** for animate objects. The pluperfect **había visto** (had seen) correctly places the last sighting before the narrative present. Compare: **primos que no había visto en años** (without **a**) — technically less precise for animate direct objects in careful written Spanish, though common in informal speech. **En años** = in years — a fixed time expression in negative contexts: **no lo había visto en años** (hadn't seen him in years).

sentences.base_language: Andrés was overwhelmed with gratitude and went around the room hugging everyone and thanking them.
sentences.target_language: Andrés se sintió desbordado de gratitud y fue por toda la sala abrazando a todos y dándoles las gracias.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Desbordado de gratitud** — **desbordar** (to overflow, to overwhelm) used as a past participle adjective: **desbordado de gratitud** (overwhelmed with gratitude, overflowing with gratitude). **Desbordar** conveys a vivid image of emotion exceeding its container. Compare: **abrumado de gratitud** (overwhelmed with gratitude — **abrumado** from **abrumar**, more focused on being burdened or crushed by something positive or negative).
**Fue por toda la sala + gerundio** — **ir por + place + gerundio** expresses movement through a space while performing an ongoing action: **fue por toda la sala abrazando** (went around the room hugging). The gerund **abrazando** describes a simultaneous, continuous action during the movement. **Dar las gracias** = to thank, to give thanks — a **dar + noun** collocation: **dándoles las gracias** (thanking them). Compare: **agradecer** (to thank, more formal): **les agradeció** (thanked them). **Dar las gracias** is more colloquial and warm; **agradecer** is slightly more formal.

sentences.base_language: The table was covered with his favourite dishes, including his mother's rice recipe that always reminded him of childhood.
sentences.target_language: La mesa estaba cubierta de sus platos favoritos, incluida la receta de arroz de su madre que siempre le recordaba a la infancia.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Estaba cubierta de** — **estar + participle** for a resulting state: **cubierta de** (covered with/in). **Cubrir de** = to cover with: **estaba cubierta de platos** (was covered in dishes). The participle **cubierta** agrees with **la mesa** (feminine singular). **Incluida** = including — past participle of **incluir** used as a conjunction or adverb, agreeing with the included noun (**la receta**, feminine singular): **incluida la receta** (including the recipe). Compare: **incluyendo** (including — gerund, invariable and equally correct): **incluyendo la receta de arroz**.
**Siempre le recordaba a la infancia** — **recordar a** = to remind of: **le recordaba a la infancia** (reminded him of childhood). **Recordar a alguien algo** and **recordarle a alguien algo** both work; here the indirect object **le** (him) marks the person reminded. The imperfect **recordaba** is used for a habitual, repeated action (the smell always brought back childhood — every time, not just once). Compare: **le recordó** (preterite — reminded him once, on this occasion).

sentences.base_language: Someone poured glasses of cava and everyone raised a toast to Andrés's health and happiness.
sentences.target_language: Alguien llenó las copas de cava y todos levantaron su copa para brindar por la salud y la felicidad de Andrés.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Llenar las copas de cava** — **llenar de** = to fill with: **llenó las copas de cava** (filled the glasses with cava). Compare: **servir cava en las copas** (to pour cava into the glasses — focusing on the pouring action). **Llenar** emphasizes the result (the glasses being full); **servir** emphasizes the act of pouring. **Las copas** = wine glasses, champagne flutes — **copa** is specifically a stemmed glass, whereas **vaso** is a tumbler or glass without a stem.
**Levantar la copa para brindar por** — **brindar por** = to toast to, to drink to: **brindaron por la salud de Andrés** (toasted Andrés's health). **Brindar** is the verb specifically for making a toast; the preposition **por** introduces the person or thing toasted. **Para brindar** expresses purpose: **levantaron la copa para brindar** (raised their glasses in order to toast). **La salud y la felicidad** — both nouns take the definite article in this fixed-phrase register: **por tu salud** (to your health), **por la felicidad** (to happiness).

sentences.base_language: Andrés gave a short, heartfelt speech and tried to put into words how much the people in that room meant to him.
sentences.target_language: Andrés dio un breve discurso emotivo e intentó expresar con palabras lo mucho que significaba para él la gente de esa sala.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Lo mucho que significaba** — the neuter article **lo** + adverb/adjective + **que** = how much, the extent to which: **lo mucho que significaban** (how much they meant). This **lo + [quantity/degree word] + que** construction is the standard way to embed a degree exclamation in a reported context: **no podía expresar lo mucho que la quería** (he couldn't express how much he loved her), **no sabes lo bien que canta** (you don't know how well she sings).
**Intentó expresar con palabras** — **intentar + infinitivo**: when the subject of both verbs is the same, the infinitive follows directly: **intentó expresar** (tried to express / put into words). Compare: **intentó que le escucharan** (tried to get them to listen — different subject, **que + subjuntivo**). **Con palabras** = in words, with words — **con** + instrument: **expresar con palabras** (to express in words), **describir con palabras** (to describe in words). **Lo mucho que significaba para él** — note **para él** (for him / to him) expressing the person for whom something has significance: **significar para alguien** (to mean something to someone).

sentences.base_language: As the evening went on, someone turned the music louder and the living room became an impromptu dance floor.
sentences.target_language: A medida que avanzaba la noche, alguien subió el volumen de la música y la sala se convirtió en una improvisada pista de baile.
sentences.grammarNote:
**A medida que avanzaba** — **a medida que + imperfect** = as, as time went on — expresses a gradual, progressive development: **a medida que avanzaba la noche** (as the evening went on). **A medida que** implies proportionality or gradual progression, making it more precise than **mientras** (while — simply simultaneous): **a medida que crecía, aprendía más** (as he grew up, he learned more). The imperfect **avanzaba** is correct for the ongoing, continuous progression of the night.
**Se convirtió en** — **convertirse en** = to become, to turn into: **se convirtió en una pista de baile** (became a dance floor). **Convertirse en** implies a transformation into a different thing or role, and is the standard verb for this type of change. Compare: **hacerse** (to become — for people or gradual personal change): **se hizo famoso** (became famous); **volverse** (to become — for changes in state or personality): **se volvió más tranquilo** (became calmer). **Improvisada** = impromptu, makeshift — agrees with **pista** (feminine singular): **una pista improvisada** (an impromptu floor).

sentences.base_language: His feet and back began to ache from dancing but he could not bring himself to stop.
sentences.target_language: Los pies y la espalda empezaron a dolerle de tanto bailar, pero no podía obligarse a parar.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Empezaron a dolerle** — gustar-type construction with **doler** + **empezar a**: the aching body parts (**los pies y la espalda**) are the grammatical subject, so the verb agrees with them (**empezaron**, third person plural). The indirect object **le** marks the person affected. **Empezar a + infinitivo** = to begin to, to start: **empezaron a dolerle** (began to ache). Compare: **le empezaron a doler** — word order is flexible; both are natural.
**De tanto bailar** — **de tanto + infinitivo** = from so much doing, from all the [activity]: **le dolían los pies de tanto bailar** (his feet ached from dancing so much). This is a very common Spanish construction expressing the cause of a physical result: **me duelen los ojos de tanto leer** (my eyes ache from reading so much), **se quedó sin voz de tanto hablar** (lost her voice from talking so much). **No podía obligarse a parar** — **obligarse a + infinitivo** = to force oneself to, to make oneself: **no podía obligarse a parar** (couldn't bring himself to stop). Compare: **no podía dejar de bailar** (couldn't stop dancing — **dejar de + infinitivo** = to stop doing).

sentences.base_language: When the birthday cake appeared — three tiers of chocolate sponge covered in candles — everyone began to sing.
sentences.target_language: Cuando apareció la tarta de cumpleaños — tres pisos de bizcocho de chocolate cubiertos de velas — todos empezaron a cantar.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Apareció la tarta** — verb-subject inversion with **aparecer**: placing **apareció** before **la tarta** is the most natural and dynamic order when introducing a new element into the narrative. This inversion is standard with **aparecer**, **llegar**, **entrar**, **salir** when the subject is introduced for the first time: **apareció un hombre** (a man appeared), **llegó la cuenta** (the bill arrived). Compare the flatter **la tarta apareció**, which is grammatically correct but less vivid.
**Tres pisos de bizcocho de chocolate cubiertos de velas** — **piso** = tier, floor (layer of a cake): **una tarta de tres pisos** (a three-tier cake). **Bizcocho** = sponge cake — the base layer of most Spanish-style cakes. **Cubiertos de velas** — participle agrees with **pisos** (masculine plural): **cubiertos** (covered). The appositive noun phrase in dashes elaborates on **la tarta**, a stylistic technique for vivid description. **Empezaron a cantar** — **empezar a + infinitivo** = to begin to do: **empezaron a cantar** (began to sing, started singing); the preterite **empezaron** marks the precise moment the singing started.

sentences.base_language: Andrés closed his eyes, made a silent wish, and blew out all the candles in a single breath.
sentences.target_language: Andrés cerró los ojos, pidió un deseo en silencio y apagó todas las velas de un solo soplido.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Pidió un deseo** — **pedir un deseo** = to make a wish (lit. to ask for a wish). In Spanish, wishes are 'asked for' with **pedir**, not 'made' with **hacer**: **pedir un deseo** is the fixed and only natural expression. Compare: *hacer un deseo* is a calque from English that sounds unnatural to native speakers. Similarly: **pedir un favor** (to ask a favour), **pedir perdón** (to ask forgiveness). The verb **pedir** covers many 'request' contexts where English uses 'make' or 'ask'.
**De un solo soplido** — **de un solo + noun** = in a single [action]: **de un solo soplido** (in a single breath/puff), **de un solo golpe** (in one blow), **de un solo vistazo** (at a single glance). The preposition **de** here expresses manner or means. **Soplido** = a puff, a blow (from **soplar** = to blow): the specific noun for the act of blowing out candles. Compare: **de un solo aliento** (in a single breath — **aliento** = breath, air from the lungs) — also used but **soplido** is more concrete and idiomatic for blowing out candles.

sentences.base_language: The cake was extraordinary — dense, moist, and layered with a cream that tasted like something from his childhood.
sentences.target_language: La tarta era extraordinaria — densa, húmeda y rellena de una crema que sabía a algo de su infancia.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Era extraordinaria** — **ser + adjective** for an inherent, defining quality: the cake's extraordinary nature is described as a characteristic property (**era** — imperfect of **ser**), not a temporary state. Compare: **estaba extraordinaria** — also possible with food to convey 'tasted/seemed extraordinary on this occasion' (**estar** for experienced quality), but **era** describes the cake's intrinsic nature. Both can be used with food; **estar** often implies a more personal, sensory impression, while **ser** describes an objective characteristic.
**Sabía a algo de su infancia** — **saber a** = to taste like, to taste of: **sabía a chocolate** (tasted of chocolate), **sabe a gloria** (tastes heavenly). **A** introduces the thing tasted: **saber a algo** (to taste like something). This is distinct from **saber** = to know (**sé la respuesta** — I know the answer). Context and the preposition **a** distinguish the two meanings. **Rellena de** = layered with, filled with — past participle adjective from **rellenar** (to fill, to stuff): **rellena de crema** (filled/layered with cream). **Húmeda** = moist — the opposite of **seco** (dry); essential vocabulary for describing baked goods.

sentences.base_language: Long after midnight, the last guests began saying their goodbyes with long embraces at the door.
sentences.target_language: Mucho después de la medianoche, los últimos invitados empezaron a despedirse con largos abrazos en la puerta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Mucho después de** = long after — **mucho** intensifies **después de** (after): **mucho después de la medianoche** (long after midnight). Compare: **poco después de** (shortly after): **poco después de las doce** (shortly after midnight). **Mucho** and **poco** are common intensifiers for temporal prepositions: **mucho antes** (long before), **poco antes** (shortly before), **mucho más tarde** (much later).
**Empezaron a despedirse** — **despedirse** = to say goodbye, to take one's leave — a reflexive verb: **despedirse de alguien** (to say goodbye to someone): **se despidió de todos** (said goodbye to everyone). The reflexive **se** is obligatory; the non-reflexive **despedir** means to fire (an employee) or to see off: **lo despidieron del trabajo** (they fired him from work), **fuimos a despedirle al aeropuerto** (we went to see him off at the airport). **Con largos abrazos** = with long embraces — **con + noun** for manner: the length and warmth of the farewell are conveyed through this adverbial phrase.

sentences.base_language: When everyone had finally left, Andrés stood alone in the quiet room, still glowing with warmth and happiness.
sentences.target_language: Cuando por fin todos se hubieron ido, Andrés se quedó solo en la sala en silencio, todavía irradiando calor y felicidad.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Cuando... se hubieron ido** — past anterior (pretérito anterior): **haber** in the preterite + past participle, used after **cuando** and similar time conjunctions to express an action completed immediately before another past action. **Se hubieron ido** (had gone, once they had gone) is the past anterior of **irse**. This tense is rare in modern spoken Spanish, where the pluperfect **se habían ido** is more common, but it appears in literary and formal written registers and is the technically precise choice after **cuando** in narration.
**Quedarse solo** — **quedarse + adjective** expresses a resulting state after a change: **se quedó solo** (was left alone, found himself alone). **Quedarse** here is distinct from **estar solo** (to be alone — static state) and **ponerse solo** (not standard); **quedarse** implies that aloneness resulted from a change (everyone leaving). Compare: **quedarse callado** (to go quiet, to remain silent), **quedarse dormido** (to fall asleep). **Irradiando** = glowing with, radiating — the gerund of **irradiar** (to radiate, to glow): **irradiando felicidad** (glowing with happiness) is a vivid and elevated choice.

sentences.base_language: He sent a long voice message to his closest friend to thank her for organizing everything.
sentences.target_language: Le envió un largo mensaje de voz a su amiga más cercana para agradecerle que hubiera organizado todo.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Para agradecerle que hubiera organizado** — **agradecer que + past subjunctive** = to be grateful that (someone did something): after **agradecer** (a verb expressing gratitude about a completed action), the past subjunctive **hubiera organizado** is required because the organizing happened before the thanking. The use of **para + infinitivo** expresses purpose (**to thank her**), and then the subjunctive clause follows the infinitive's implicit subject: **para agradecerle [a ella] que hubiera organizado** (to thank her for having organized).
**Mensaje de voz** = voice message — a compound noun: **de voz** (of voice) specifies the type of message. Compare: **nota de voz** (voice note — used interchangeably in many varieties of Spanish). **Le envió... a su amiga** — double marking of the indirect object: **le** (to her) is the indirect object pronoun, reinforced by **a su amiga más cercana**. This doubling is standard in Spanish when the indirect object is specified as a noun or proper name; omitting **le** in this context would sound incomplete. **Hubiera organizado** is the past subjunctive (imperfect subjunctive) of **haber** + past participle, forming the pluperfect subjunctive.

sentences.base_language: Lying in bed that night, he thought that this was exactly the kind of evening that stayed with you for the rest of your life.
sentences.target_language: Tumbado en la cama aquella noche, pensó que era exactamente el tipo de velada que se te quedaba para el resto de la vida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Se te quedaba** — **quedarse** in an impersonal construction with **se te**: **quedarse** here means to stay (with you), to remain (in your memory). The **se** is the impersonal or generalizing **se**, and **te** is the indirect object (you — generalizing the experience to any person). This combination **se te queda** (it stays with you) is a natural colloquial expression: **eso se te queda grabado** (that stays etched in your mind), **la canción se le quedó** (the song stayed with him). The imperfect **quedaba** conveys the habitual nature of this type of evening.
**Tumbado en la cama** — **tumbado/a** = lying down, stretched out — past participle of **tumbar** (to knock down, to lay down) used as an adjective describing position: **tumbado en la cama** (lying in bed). Compare: **acostado** (lying down, in bed — often implying one has gone to bed): **estaba acostado** (was in bed/lying down). **Tumbado** is more neutral and physical; **acostado** implies the full bedtime routine. **Aquella noche** = that night — **aquel/aquella** (that — distant demonstrative) in narrative contexts often signals nostalgia or distance from the present: **aquella noche** (that night — in the story) vs. **esa noche** (that night — closer reference).
